title: Chocolate toffee bark
subtitle: Dark chocolate bark topped with homemade toffee shards and sea salt
excerpt: Dark chocolate melted and spread thin, topped with brittle homemade toffee shards and flaky sea salt before it sets. The contrast of bitter chocolate, sweet crunchy toffee, and salt is what makes it worth the small effort involved. Breaks into irregular pieces for sharing. Takes 45 minutes including setting time.
sourceNotes: Chocolate bark (melted chocolate spread thin and topped with confectionery additions before setting) is a domestic confectionery format documented in American and British baking literature from the mid-twentieth century. The combination of dark chocolate and toffee shards (in the style of a Daim or Heath bar) is widely documented in home confectionery recipes. The recipe here synthesises the established domestic formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed bag. Eat from frozen or thaw for 10 minutes at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a cool, dry place for up to three weeks. Do not refrigerate in warm weather — condensation causes the chocolate to bloom.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required. The toffee is cooked on the hob to hard-crack stage at around 155°C. A sugar thermometer is recommended; without one, test by dropping a little into cold water — it should shatter immediately.

glossaryTerms.definition: Chocolate bark is made by spreading melted chocolate thinly across a lined tray and pressing toppings into the surface before it sets. The chocolate must be spread to about 5 mm — thinner is fragile and snaps unexpectedly; thicker does not give the characteristic clean break of good bark. Toppings go on while the chocolate is still wet and warm, so they bond to the surface. Once set, the slab is broken freehand into irregular pieces.

content.text: Make the toffee first and the
after. The toffee shards can be prepared an hour or a day ahead. If hot toffee or caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Line two baking trays with baking paper. Make the toffee: melt the sugar, butter, and golden syrup in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Stop stirring and cook until the mixture reaches 155°C on a sugar thermometer — the caramel will be a deep amber. If hot toffee touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Pour immediately onto one of the lined trays, tilting to spread it thin. Leave to cool completely and harden, then break into shards of roughly 1 to 2 cm.

Melt the dark chocolate in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Pour onto the second lined tray and spread to about 5 mm thickness with a palette knife. While the chocolate is still wet, scatter the toffee shards over the surface, pressing them lightly in. Sprinkle over the sea salt flakes. Leave at room temperature for 30 minutes, or refrigerate for 15 minutes, until completely set. Break into irregular pieces by hand.
